event: Neolithic farming communities settle the fertile river valleys of the region, leaving early settlements such as those at Anzabegovo and Govrlevo.
The Tumba Madžari Neolithic settlement near Skopje flourishes, producing distinctive figurines including the 'Great Mother' goddess.
Paeonian and other Bronze Age tribes inhabit the central Balkan region that will later become Macedonia.
Traditional founding of the Argead dynasty of the ancient kingdom of Macedon under Caranus, beginning Macedonian royal history.
Paeonian kingdoms emerge in the Vardar valley, dominating the region north of ancient Macedon.
The Persian Empire under Darius I extends influence over the region, making Macedon a Persian vassal during the Greco-Persian conflicts.
Philip II ascends the Macedonian throne and begins transforming Macedon into a dominant Balkan power.
Philip II subdues the Paeonians, incorporating much of the central region into the expanding kingdom of Macedon.
Alexander the Great becomes king of Macedon and launches the conquests that spread Hellenistic culture across Asia.
Alexander the Great dies in Babylon, and his vast empire fragments among his successors, the Diadochi.
Rome defeats Perseus of Macedon at the Battle of Pydna, ending the Antigonid dynasty and Macedonian independence.
Macedonia is formally annexed as a Roman province after the suppression of Andriscus's revolt.
The Apostle Paul preaches in the region, founding early Christian communities at Philippi and elsewhere in Roman Macedonia.
On the division of the Roman Empire, the region falls under the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire.
Slavic tribes migrate into and settle the region, profoundly reshaping its ethnic and linguistic character.
Saints Cyril and Methodius, born in Thessalonica, develop the Glagolitic alphabet, laying foundations for Slavic literacy.
Saint Clement of Ohrid establishes the Ohrid Literary School, a major center of Slavic learning and the Cyrillic tradition.
Tsar Samuel establishes his powerful Bulgarian state with Ohrid as a key capital and seat of its patriarchate.
Byzantine Emperor Basil II crushes Samuel's army at the Battle of Kleidion, leading to Byzantine reconquest of the region.
Stefan Dušan is crowned Emperor of the Serbs and Greeks at Skopje, making the region part of the powerful Serbian Empire.
Ottoman forces capture Skopje, beginning more than five centuries of Ottoman rule over the region.
The Karposh Uprising against Ottoman rule briefly liberates Kratovo and Kumanovo before being suppressed.
The Ottoman authorities abolish the autocephalous Archbishopric of Ohrid, subordinating local Christians to the Greek Patriarchate.
The Internal Macedonian Revolutionary Organization (IMRO) is founded in Thessaloniki to fight for autonomy from the Ottomans.
The Ilinden Uprising erupts and the short-lived Kruševo Republic is proclaimed before Ottoman forces crush the rebellion.
The First Balkan War breaks out, with Balkan allies driving the Ottomans from most of the region.
The Treaty of Bucharest partitions the region of Macedonia among Serbia, Greece, and Bulgaria after the Second Balkan War.
The Vardar region is incorporated into the new Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (later Yugoslavia).
The area is organized as the Vardar Banovina, an administrative province of the Kingdom of Yugoslavia.
Axis forces occupy Yugoslav Macedonia in World War II, with most of the territory administered by Bulgaria.
The Anti-Fascist Assembly (ASNOM) proclaims the People's Republic of Macedonia, declaring the modern Macedonian state and standardizing the language.
Macedonia becomes one of the six constituent republics of socialist Yugoslavia under Josip Broz Tito.
A devastating earthquake destroys much of Skopje, killing over a thousand people and prompting international reconstruction aid.
The Macedonian Orthodox Church unilaterally declares its autocephaly from the Serbian Orthodox Church.
Macedonia holds a referendum and declares independence from Yugoslavia, peacefully and without armed conflict.
Kiro Gligorov becomes the first president of independent Macedonia as the new state establishes its institutions.
The country joins the United Nations under the provisional name 'the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia' amid a naming dispute with Greece.
Macedonia and Greece sign an Interim Accord easing the naming dispute, and Macedonia adopts a new flag after a Greek economic embargo.
An armed insurgency by ethnic Albanian rebels ends with the Ohrid Framework Agreement, granting greater rights to the Albanian minority.
Macedonia is granted official candidate status for membership in the European Union.
Greece blocks Macedonia's NATO accession bid at the Bucharest summit over the unresolved name dispute.
Macedonia and Greece sign the Prespa Agreement, agreeing to rename the country the Republic of North Macedonia.
The country officially renames itself the Republic of North Macedonia, resolving the decades-long dispute with Greece.
North Macedonia becomes the 30th member of NATO, its first major Euro-Atlantic integration milestone.
The European Union formally opens accession negotiations with North Macedonia after years of delay.
Gordana Siljanovska-Davkova is elected the country's first female president as the VMRO-DPMNE party returns to power.
